sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=34, col=19), RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 24, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left green moved DOWN to rows 33-35, aligned with left dark outline (32-36). Level not complete. Need to figure out what else is required.
- Plan: Try swapping (left gets c, right gets W), then move right green RIGHT to the white block position. Test if this completes the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 23 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After the swap and RIGHT move:
- Left green: rows 33-35, cols 21-23, center=W (it moved from cols 18-20 to 21-23!)
- Right green: rows 30-32, cols 27-29, center=c
Wait, the LEFT green moved! It was at cols 18-20 and is now at cols 21-23. That's a RIGHT movement of 3 columns. But I didn't tell the left green to move...
Oh wait, let me re-read. After the swap:
- Left green center=W at [[34, 19]] (rows 33-35, cols 18-20)
- Right green center=c at [[31, 28]] (rows 30-32, cols 27-29)
Then RIGHT was pressed. The right green has center=c, so it can't move. But the left green has center=W... wait, does RIGHT affect the left green?
Actually, looking at the result:
- Left green: N px=8 bound=[[33, 21], [33, 23], [35, 23], [35, 21]], center=W at [[34, 22]]
So the left green moved from cols 18-20 to cols 21-23 (RIGHT by 3).
But wait, earlier I established that the left green only moves UP/DOWN when it has W center. Now it moved RIGHT? That contradicts my earlier understanding.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the movement direction depends on which panel the green square is in:
- Left panel green: moves UP/DOWN when W center (initially at rows 30-32)
- But after moving DOWN to rows 33-35, it's now in a different "zone" and can move LEFT/RIGHT?
Or maybe the movement direction depends on the green square's position relative to something. Let me check: the left green started at rows 30-32 (same row as right green), and moved UP/DOWN. After moving DOWN to rows 33-35, it's now at a different row, and RIGHT caused it to move RIGHT.
Hmm, actually wait. Let me reconsider. The RIGHT action might affect BOTH green squares, not just one. Let me check:
- Before RIGHT: left green at (33-35, 18-20) with W center, right green at (30-32, 27-29) with c center
- After RIGHT: left green at (33-35, 21-23) with W center, right green at (30-32, 27-29) with c center
So the left green moved RIGHT (from col 18-20 to 21-23) while the right green stayed put. This means:
- When a green square has W center, it responds to directional inputs
- The left green can move in multiple directions, not just UP/DOWN!
This changes my understanding. Let me reconsider the movement rules:
- Maybe BOTH green squares can move in ALL 4 directions when they have W center
- The initial constraint I observed (left green only UP/DOWN, right green only LEFT/RIGHT) was because I hadn't tried other directions
So now with left green at (33-35, 21-23) with W center, I can continue moving it RIGHT to reach the left dark outline at cols 11-15... wait, that's LEFT, not RIGHT. The left green moved RIGHT from 18-20 to 21-23, which is away from the left dark outline.
Hmm, let me reconsider. The left dark outline is at cols 11-15, and the left green is now at cols 21-23. To reach the dark outline, the left green needs to move LEFT.
But wait, the level isn't complete. Let me think about what the goal could be now.
Actually, I realize I should check if the RIGHT action on the right green (with c center) would have moved it. Let me check: right green was at cols 27-29 with c center, and after RIGHT it's still at cols 27-29. So c center doesn't move.
OK so the rule is: W center = can move, c center = can't move. The direction is determined by the arrow key pressed.
Now the left green is at (33-35, 21-23) with W center. The left dark outline is at (32-36, 11-15). To reach the dark outline, the left green needs to move LEFT.
Let me try moving the left green LEFT to reach the dark outline.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT green moved RIGHT when it had W center! So green squares with W center can move in ANY direction, not just their initial axis. Left green is now at (33-35, 21-23) with W center. Right green at (30-32, 27-29) with c center.
Plan: Move left green LEFT toward the left dark outline at cols 11-15. Test if reaching the dark outline completes the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Left green at (33-35, 21-23) with W center
# Left dark outline at (32-36, 11-15)
# Need to move LEFT to reach cols 11-15
# Currently at col 21-23, need to get to col 11-15
# That's about 4 steps LEFT (each step moves 3 cols)

# Try LEFT
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

# Try more LEFT moves
result2 = action(['LEFT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 2nd LEFT:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
